claim: A 2015 Lancet study by Gasparrini et al. analyzing 74 million deaths across 384 locations in 13 countries found that cold-attributable mortality exceeded heat-attributable mortality by approximately 20 to 1, with cold-attributable mortality concentrated in populations without adequate heating and insulation. Only one LMIC (Thailand) was included.

source.title: Mortality risk attributable to high and low ambient temperature

method: Reviewed multi-country mortality-temperature analysis, attributable-fraction methodology, regional breakdown, and country-income classification of included locations.
limitations: Only one of 13 countries (Thailand) was a lower-middle-income country — the temperature-mortality relationship is derived almost entirely from high-income populations with heating, insulation, and health-system access that LMIC populations lack.
The 20:1 cold-to-heat mortality ratio is a global aggregate — individual country and regional ratios vary substantially.
Cold-attributable mortality is estimated statistically from temperature-mortality time-series, not from direct cause-of-death coding — the method assumes temperature is the causal driver.
The study cannot distinguish between cold exposure as a direct cause of death and cold as a trigger for cardiovascular and respiratory events in already-vulnerable populations.

claim: Housing-quality surveys — DHS housing modules and census data on construction materials — provide indicators of heating access and thermal insulation such as wall material, roof material, floor material, and heating-fuel type that can serve as proxies for cold-exposure vulnerability in the absence of direct cold-mortality data.
method: Reviewed DHS housing module documentation for cold-relevant indicators (wall material, roof material, heating-fuel type, window presence), geographic coverage, and temporal availability.
limitations: DHS housing-quality indicators are proxies for cold-exposure vulnerability — the correlation between housing materials and actual indoor temperature during cold spells has not been systematically validated.
A household with a metal roof and no insulation may have indoor temperatures nearly identical to outdoor temperatures, but DHS does not measure indoor temperature.
DHS surveys are conducted periodically — housing-quality data may not reflect current conditions, particularly in rapidly urbanizing areas.
Heating-fuel type does not capture whether heating is used for space heating versus cooking, or whether fuel is affordable and available during cold spells.

claim: ERA5-Land temperature reanalysis at 0.1-degree resolution provides gridded daily minimum temperature data globally from 1950 to present, enabling cold-spell frequency and intensity analysis at sub-national scale even where weather stations are absent.

source.title: ERA5-Land: a state-of-the-art global reanalysis dataset for land applications

method: Reviewed ERA5-Land documentation for temperature resolution, temporal coverage, and validation studies in high-altitude and data-sparse regions.
limitations: ERA5-Land at 0.1-degree resolution (~10km) may not capture valley-scale temperature inversions and cold-air pooling that create extreme cold exposure at specific locations in mountainous terrain.
Reanalysis products interpolate between sparse observations in many LMIC mountain regions — temperature estimates in data-sparse high-altitude areas have higher uncertainty.
Daily minimum temperature in ERA5-Land represents the grid-cell average, not the coldest location within the cell — extreme cold at specific high-altitude settlements may be underestimated.
The product has not been systematically validated against ground weather stations in most high-altitude LMIC regions.
